summary: Jeff Atwood argues that users don't read dialog boxes not because they're stupid, but because they've been trained by years of experience that dialogs are productivity-blocking obstacles with meaningless technical jargon. He cites Joel Spolsky and Eric Lippert to explain how users rationally learn to dismiss dialogs as quickly as possible since most provide no useful information. The post concludes that any time software asks users to make choices they don't care about, the software has failed. Well-designed applications should aim for zero dialog boxes by anticipating needs, remembering preferences, and automatically protecting users from mistakes.
key_points: Users are trained by experience to ignore dialog boxes because they're modal interruptions filled with incomprehensible technical jargon
From a user's perspective, dialog boxes are productivity impediments that provide no useful information - just obstacles to dismiss
Any time you ask users to make a choice they don't care about, you've failed them as a designer
Well-designed software anticipates needs, remembers preferences, and automatically protects users rather than asking questions
The goal should be writing entire applications free of dialog boxes
money_quotes: There are quite a few users who simply do not read words that you put on the screen. If you pop up an error box of any sort, they simply will not read it.
It's not that users are morons or that they 'forget' to think. It's that users are trained to not think.
Users do not think of them as 'modal,' they think of them as 'preventing me from getting any work done until I get rid of them.'
Dialog boxes usually say 'If you want to tech the tech, you need to tech the tech with the teching tech tech. Tech the tech? Yes / No.'
From a user perspective, dialog boxes are impediments to productivity which provide no information.
Any time you're asking the user to make a choice they don't care about, you have failed the user.
The goal should be to write an entire application free of dialog boxes.
key_insight: Users rationally ignore dialog boxes because software has trained them that dialogs are meaningless obstacles, so good software should eliminate them entirely by anticipating needs and automating protection.
